data.title: Microsoft: More Resilient To Trade Wars Than Peers
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It has been a while since I last covered Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT). My previous bullish article on MSFT was published in early August, and the stock's total return slightly outpaced the broader market's growth since then. My stance remains bullish due to Microsoft's intact strategic positioning, which is fortified by the robust ongoing secular digitalization trend. In addition, the stock remains very attractively valued. All in all, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for MSFT.

Recent Developments

The company's latest quarterly earnings were released on April 30, accompanied by a robust double beat against consensus forecasts. A big bullish indication is the fact that MSFT's FQ3 2025 YoY revenue growth accelerated to 13.3%, compared to the 12.3% growth delivered in FQ2 2025.

The impressive YoY adjusted EPS expansion is also another vital bullish indicator. Microsoft's non-GAAP EPS achieved a record level of $3.46 in FQ3 2025, which is significantly higher compared to $2.94 generated during the same quarter of FY2024.

The quality of the EPS expansion always matters a lot to me because this is the primary indication of the company's ability to demonstrate operating leverage through cross-selling and the economies of scale effect. Despite MSFT's vast scale, the company continues delivering operating margin expansion. The YoY increase from 44.6% to 45.7% looks very impressive. As a result, Microsoft's free cash flow [FCF] generating capacity remains intact. This factor supports the company's fortress financial position, providing more opportunities to finance growth projects. The last but not least significant bullish indicator from the latest earnings release is that MSFT demonstrated confident growth across all segments, which indicates an extremely strong business mix.

I would like to remind readers about Microsoft's unique positioning in the AI revolution, as it has a very close strategic partnership with OpenAI. OpenAI owns ChatGPT, the world's by far most popular chatbot that commands almost a 60% market share. In addition, Microsoft's own chatbot solution, Copilot, is also among the largest players by the market share. Having such a solid exposure to the thriving generative AI field combined with Microsoft's massive 21% market share in cloud is a solid blend to maximize the company's cross-selling potential.

The upcoming earnings release is scheduled for late July, and the setup looks bullish. First, the company's FQ4 2025 YoY revenue growth is expected to accelerate again to around 14%. Second, the adjusted EPS is expected to slightly outpace revenue growth, which once again proves MSFT's massive operating leverage. Third, there were significantly more upward EPS revisions than downgrades over the last 90 days, indicating extremely strong Wall Street sentiment regarding MSFT's ability to deliver strong earnings.

My optimism is also backed by several bullish outlook reiterations from prominent Wall Street analysts during May. Several largest firms [like Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, Morgan Stanley] remain bullish on Microsoft's future prospects, which is always a positive sign for me. On average, Wall Street analysts believe that MSFT's fair share price is above $500.

Finally, I think that Microsoft is the best positioned in the current uncertain environment caused by global trade tensions if we are speaking about the world's three largest tech companies. The fact that Microsoft's software-centric business model ["Windows and Devices" sales represented only around 6% of the total revenue during the nine months ended March 31, 2025] is not significantly dependent on imports and exports is a great advantage in the current environment. For example, we see that two other giants, Apple or Nvidia, face quite notable challenges in the current U.S.-China trade war because their business models are much more dependent on selling physical products. This gives MSFT investors another crucial advantage and protection against global trade war risks.

Valuation Update

The discounted cash flow [DCF] model is my favorite tool to value large growth stocks because long-term revenue projections are usually quite reliable, and there is much more predictability in the future earnings growth trajectory. The DCF approach starts with figuring out the discount rate, which is the company's cost of equity due to the low reliance on debt. The CAPM approach suggests that the discount rate is 8.73%, which I consider reasonable.

The first-year FCF margin assumption is always conservative in all my DCF models because future growth is not guaranteed [despite being highly probable]. Therefore, I start with calculating the ex-stock-based compensation [ex-SBC] margin. According to the TTM metrics, MSFT's ex-SBC FCF margin is 16%. This is the year-one assumption, expected to demonstrate growth by 100-bps per year due to MSFT's historically strong operating leverage.

Overall, the long-term revenue growth trajectory suggested by consensus is reasonable, as on average, it incorporates revenue growth rate deceleration over time. Therefore, for MSFT's DCF model, I relied on consensus estimates. As a result, DCF's revenue CAGR until FY2032 is around 11%, which looks conservative enough for the tech behemoth like MSFT. A 5% growth rate was applied to MSFT's termination value due to the company's strong positioning to drive both organic [acquiring new customers and cross-selling potential] and inorganic [historically strong M&A record] growth over the long term.

The business's fair value is close to $4 trillion, according to my DCF model. With the current market cap of circa $3.4 trillion, MSFT is 17% undervalued. In the second scenario, I want to demonstrate that the market currently assumes a 4.3% perpetual growth rate, which is unfairly conservative for a company like Microsoft. I think that this is unfair due to the double-digit revenue CAGR observed over the last decade, plus the projected aggressive EPS and revenue growth for the next ten years.

To wrap up, I want to outline the target price calculation in the final valuation working. Multiplying the current share price of around $460 by 1.17 [which incorporates the aforementioned 17% upside potential] gives me the target price of $538.

Risks Update

Competition in the cloud industry remains fierce as the race involves other giants like Google and Amazon. All three cloud giants demonstrate aggressive revenue growth rates, which clearly indicate that the competition is poised to remain intense. Moreover, all three behemoths are approximately equally aggressive in their AI infrastructure investments. This means that the balance sheet strength is crucial because data centers require multi-billion investments and Microsoft looks notably weaker than Google from this perspective. Please do not get me wrong here, Microsoft's financial position is a fortress with almost $80 billion in cash and quite low leverage. But Google's financial position is even stronger.

In addition, I think we all remember last year's sharp MSFT sell-off, which was triggered by the global outage in Microsoft's services. Despite the fact that this outage was not Microsoft's fault but due to unforeseen issues during CrowdStrike's (CRWD) software update, the situation significantly damaged Microsoft's reputation. Microsoft's dominance is not only the company's large asset but also means extremely high expectations regarding the reliability of its services. Thus, there are substantial risks related to potential outages of Microsoft's services, even if they are caused by external partners.

According to last decade's by-month seasonality chart, June has been historically one of the weakest months for MSFT, with just a 50% win rate. Therefore, investors might see some share price weakness next month. On the other hand, July is historically strong, which is highly likely related to the calendar Q2 earnings release. Therefore, dollar-cost averaging might be a prudent option to further accumulate the MSFT position.

Bottom Line

To conclude, MSFT is still a "Strong Buy". The current share price of around $460 looks like a great buying opportunity, given a substantial upside potential suggested by my valuation update. Last but not least, Microsoft's fundamentals remain intact amid the current thriving digital environment.

data.title: When $7 Trillion Wakes Up: Buy These 3 Dividend Stocks Before Everyone Else Does
data.description:
Introduction

Let's start this article on an incredibly silly note.

Did you know the thickness of a single dollar bill is 0.0043 inches? It's also 6.14 inches long and 2.61 inches wide. It also weighs 1 gram.

An official NFL football field used for NFL games is 360 feet long and 160 feet wide. This translates to 4,320 and 1,920 inches, respectively.

Based on these numbers, we would have to spend $517,574 if we wanted to cover the entire field with single-dollar bills. A trillion dollars would translate to a stack of 185.5 feet or 56.5 meters.

And yes, I am fully aware that the picture I am using above is from a college team, which plays on fields with a slight difference from NFL fields.

However, it's a better picture than any free NFL stadium picture I could find, and I honestly doubt anyone cares, as nothing I have said so far is of any importance.

All we have established is that $1 trillion is a lot of dollars.

I had to bring it up, as America's money market funds currently hold $7.2 trillion in assets, the highest number ever, and the result of one of the most consistent periods of inflows since the 1990s, as the chart below shows.

As of the second quarter, total MMF holdings are 13% higher compared to the prior-year quarter. This marks five consecutive quarters of double-digit growth.

I cannot say I am surprised that assets under management have risen so fast.

Despite a number of Fed rate cuts and the related decline in short-term government debt interest rates, the 3-month bill still yields roughly 4.0%, a number that competes with the yields on famous dividend ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM), which yield 4.0% and 3.0%, respectively.

When adding elevated economic uncertainty and the fact that many people close to or in retirement have made 'a lot of' money in the market in recent decades, it makes sense that many opt to buy risk-free 4% income.

Also, some sources make the case that banks don't really care anymore that money is flowing into MMFs. They have plenty of liquidity and a hard time competing with the elevated yields the market offers. They would rather focus on keeping yields low on products like checking accounts.

It also helps that when it comes to offering high yields, MMFs are much more flexible, as they can quickly lower rates the moment short-term bond yields come down.

With that said, there are some downsides.

For starters, Jason Zweig made the case earlier this month that "Your Money-Market Fund Is Ripping You Off," which was the title of his thought-provoking article.

He explained that, in general, costs have come down. In the 1990s, mutual funds still used to cost 1% to 2% per year. Nowadays, we can buy ETFs with total expense ratios of 0.05%. The average mutual fund costs 0.33%.

Money Market Funds have become more expensive since 2021, as we can see below.

This development is interesting, as the Fed has kept rates close to zero most of the time between 2008 and 2021. During this period, most Money Market Funds would have had negative yields after costs. That's why managers waived more than $50 billion in fees, according to the article.

Now, with trillions of new capital, they feel like they deserve a bigger share of the pie. Also, because ETF providers did not really care for the MMF space, mutual funds had little to no competition from low-cost ETFs.

The interesting thing is that this is changing now, as new entrants are storming the market, which means two things:

  • New entrants are cheaper, with expense ratios of only 0.2%, compared to 0.5% for the average MMF.
  • Putting the current 0.5% costs into perspective, it makes the 3.9% gross yield much less interesting.

Only last September did Dallas-based Texas Capital Bank Private Wealth Advisors succeed in launching the first money-market ETF. It quickly grew to $40 million, thanks to low fees and high yields. BlackRock’s iShares followed this February with two money-market ETFs of its own.

Texas Capital Government Money Market and the iShares ETFs, Prime Money Market and Government Money Market, all charge annual fees of only 0.2%; Texas Capital yields 4.29% and the iShares funds 4.31% and 4.16%, respectively. - WSJ (Jason Zweig)

Moreover, existing players aren't just more expensive, but they also get support from brokers like Schwab and Fidelity, which boosted the two ETFs mentioned in the quote above off their platform.

They do this, as they both prefer offering their own, in-house solutions. It also helps them protect their income from fees, as both brokers have more expensive money market solutions.

I am bringing all of this up because I believe Money Market Funds could become much less attractive in the quarters and years ahead.

For example, the net yield on many money market funds will be unable to compete with high-quality dividend stocks after another two rate cuts of 50 basis points.

For that, we would need a few things, including a sign of improving economic growth and lower trade war tensions. Given current market dynamics, the only thing that is keeping the Fed from lowering rates is trade-related inflation fears.

While I believe the next economic upswing will cause another rally in inflation rates due to secular factors like supply chain reconfigurations, labor shortages, the end of the shale oil boom, and others, the short-term development of lower rates could push hundreds of billions, if not trillions, into alternatives.

These alternatives are high-quality dividend stocks.

Using the SCHD ETF as a proxy for these stocks again, we see that short-term bonds only yield marginally higher, which I expect to flip soon.

If that happens, I expect a lot of high-quality dividend stocks to see a steep decline in yields, as a lot of new capital inflows would support their stock prices.

This is both good and bad news.

  • It's good because it would likely give many of our investments a decent tailwind.
  • It's bad because if we want to deploy capital in the future, we'll likely have to take on a lot more risk, as the best dividend stocks will all have lower yields.

In general, this is a trend I will discuss a lot more in the future, especially because this applies to a lot of stocks.

However, as I already have your attention, I'll use the second part of this article to present three stocks that I expect to see substantial gains if a money market rotation were to happen.

Lockheed Martin (LMT) - Guess Who's Back?

Not a single stock in my portfolio has annoyed me more than Lockheed Martin in recent years.

What a way to start a bullish thesis on the company, right?

After consistently outperforming the market since the early 2000s, the ratio between Lockheed Martin stock and the S&P 500 has entered a very volatile downtrend since the pandemic, as America's largest pure-play defense contractor has been suffering from post-pandemic supply chain issues, inflation, budget uncertainties,

Because of these issues, I often thought about selling the company.

However, every time I spent at least a few minutes researching the company, I came back to my original conclusion, which is that Lockheed remains a great anti-cyclical dividend grower, especially at current prices.

While new entrants are certainly a risk factor, I believe fears of Lockheed (and its peers) becoming obsolete are overblown. Not a single new entrant has the capabilities that Lockheed has when it comes to taking on large-scale projects in areas like aeronautics and missile defense. These operations are capital-intensive, require decades of experience, and a strong supply chain.

Lockheed has all of this.

It also has long-term tailwinds from defense modernization in the United States, Europe, and other partners across the globe.

On top of that, it is one of the potential winners of the Golden Dome program, which could become a trillion-dollar endeavor, as I wrote in a recent article.

Although I did not make Lockheed one of my two Golden Dome winner picks, it would have been my third pick, as the company has key operations in all areas that matter, including command, control, and communication systems, its Patriot Advanced Capability missiles ("PACs"), Space-Based Infrared System satellites, and many other programs.

When it comes to new entrants, the company's main goal is to learn from these players, adopting innovative business models while leveraging its own history of innovation, including its Skunk Works division.

Partnerships include the one with the German Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) company. While this is hardly a new entrant, it allows Lockheed to win bigger deals in Europe, including in missile defense.

Moreover:

  • The company is looking to become leaner, which includes creating joint ventures to speed up innovation and get solutions to market faster.
  • It is aggressively investing in autonomous systems. This is key, as most new entrants are focused on this as well (think of Anduril).
  • Lockheed is looking into software as a standalone offering, potentially as an SaaS model. Essentially, this shift reflects market trends toward decoupling hardware and software, which better aligns with customer needs for agility and cost-effectiveness. Here, I expect major cooperation with larger software players to occur as well.

With that said, the risk/reward has gotten great. Currently yielding 2.8%, Lockheed has a 44% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 7%. It has hiked its dividend for 22 consecutive years.

We also see a much better growth outlook. After 20% EPS contraction in 2024, analysts finally see a path to consistent growth, as this year is expected to see 22% growth, potentially followed by 10% and 8% growth in 2026 and 207, respectively.

Applying a 19.0x multiple, we get a price target of $613, roughly 30% above the current price.

Hence, on top of improved growth, I expect a potential rotation into high-quality dividend stocks to lead to a much lower yield, making the current 2.8% yield a great deal for investors seeking a mix of growth and income.

AbbVie (ABBV) - The Healthcare Giant That Keeps Delivering

When it comes to high-quality dividend stocks, AbbVie deserves to be on the list.

The company was one of the first higher-yielding dividend stocks I bought after the pandemic, as I loved the mix of growth and income it provided.

Moreover, after losing Humira exclusivity in 2023, the company has proven to be capable of using M&A and organic growth to fill the gap and return to elevated long-term growth, an achievement that has allowed it to return 148% over the past five years, beating the market by roughly 40 points.

From 2012 to 2020, Humira was the best-selling drug on the planet. Now, the company is focused on Skyrizi and Rinvoq, two blockbuster drugs in immunology, which are expected to generate a combined $31 billion in 2027 revenues.

Moreover, the absence of major patent expirations until 2030 provides near-term stability, although significant expirations for Skyrizi, Rinvoq, Vraylar, Imbruvica, and Venclexta are expected between 2030 and 2035, which makes it critical to continue pipeline development and acquisitions.

Hence, since early 2024, the company has executed 24 M&A deals to improve its pipeline and offset future patent loss risks.

On top of that, there are policy risks, as President Trump signed the Most Favored Nation ("MFN") executive order, which aims to bring down the costs of drugs in the United States to levels seen in other developed nations.

For starters, this plan is in its early stages, as it is hard to compare costs in the U.S. to European costs. While prices in Europe are often perceived as 20% to 40% lower, the differences do not include rebates, Medicaid penalties, and IRA negotiations.

Also, Medicaid's low net prices due to CPI penalties do not typically influence commercial negotiations. Hence, AbbVie expects minimal spillover from MFN, which is consistent with its view on IRA-negotiated prices, though payers may still push for concessions.

It also believes it can mitigate the risks of restricted direct-to-consumer marketing initiatives from Robert F. Kennedy Jr. If advertising were to be limited, the company would pivot to disease awareness campaigns and digital channels.

With regard to its dividend, AbbVie yields 3.6%. This dividend has a 60% payout ratio, a five-year CAGR of 7.2%, and uninterrupted annual dividend hikes since its spin-off from Abbott Laboratories (ABT).

Going forward, analysts expect 21% EPS growth in 2025 to be followed by 14% and 10% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively. Applying a 15x multiple, we get a conservative annual return outlook of at least 12% by including its dividend.

If AbbVie is able to execute on its plans and use M&A to improve its portfolio, I believe it is set for a few more decades of elevated EPS growth, despite policy risks.

In the event of a rotation from cash to equities, I believe ABBV will be a prime target, as it has both growth and income without cyclical risks.

And, just like Lockheed, it has a credit rating of A-.

VICI Properties (VICI) - All It Needs Is Rates To Come Down

VICI Properties is one of the REITs I bring up every now and then, as I believe it's one of the best income stocks in its industry.

For starters, it is one of the very few REITs with a moat, as this triple-net lease REIT generates roughly half of its annual rent on the Las Vegas Strip, where it owns non-commoditized buildings that are, as the name suggests, very hard to compete with.

Unlike a random Walgreens, Dollar General, or similar operators that can move across the street if they find an empty space with better rates, its tenants in Las Vegas rely on their resorts, which make them competitive.

As a result, more than 80% of its rent comes from Caesars Entertainment (CZR), MGM Resorts (MGM), and the Las Vegas Venetian, which alone pays more than $300 million in annual rent.

The problem is that growing in Vegas is hard. After all, new construction of resorts is limited, and the market is super competitive, with major players like Blackstone (BX) and other mega money managers all competing for a stake in major casinos.

But that's OK, as VICI has focused on all kinds of entertainment, having realized that despite AI, e-commerce, and other trends, humanity still wants to go out and have fun.

That's why I am so glad the company is also increasingly good at communicating its intent to shareholders, including the presentation of "life cycle" experiences that include casino resorts, luxury lifestyle hubs, amateur sports, wellness, gold, and more.

Hence, what initially started as a company managing Caesars' assets after its bankruptcy is now a real estate giant focusing on a wide range of entertainment assets run by highly reliable operators.

For example, on top of working closely on the new luxury One Beverly Hills in Los Angeles, which I consider a fantastic deal, the company announced a significant new partnership with Red Rock Resorts, which is a major gaming, development, and management company.

On April 4, 2025, VICI committed up to $510 million in a delayed draw term loan facility to fund the development of the North Fork Mono Casino Resort on tribal land in Central California. According to VICI, this marks its first gaming investment on tribal land and its second overall tribal land investment, following a loan for a Great Wolf Lodge project in 2023.

The project is expected to be operational in September 2026 and come with 2,400 slot machines, 40 table games, high-class dining, retail, and fantastic accessibility.

These are the kind of deals I want to see much more of, especially because they come with relationships that pave the road for opportunities like sale-leaseback transactions.

All of these deals are protected by its investment-grade balance sheet, which comes with a BBB- credit rating, a net leverage ratio of 5.3x, within its target range of 5.0x to 5.5x EBITDA.

Moreover, while other companies are struggling with tariffs, VICI raised its full-year AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) guidance in 1Q25 to the range of $2.47 billion to $2.50 billion. On a per-share basis, AFFO growth is expected to be 3.8% this year.

This is good news for its dividend and its valuation.

Currently yielding 5.5%, the dividend has a five-year CAGR of 7.7% and a payout ratio of 75%. Going forward, I expect mid-single-digit annual dividend growth to last.

With regard to its valuation, we're dealing with a company trading at just 13.7x AFFO. This is below its multi-decade multiple of 15.7x.

A certain discount is warranted, as we are now in a higher-yielding environment. As we discussed in the first part of this article, the higher the rates on risk-free alternatives, the lower the average REIT valuations.

However, that's exactly why I expect a lot of value to be unlocked if rates decline, which could unlock 13-14% annual returns in a conservative scenario of mid-single digit per-share AFFO growth, its 5.5% dividend, and a gradual return to a 15-17x multiple.

If I had a more income-focused portfolio, I would own VICI.

Takeaway

This article started with a ridiculous dollar-bill math exercise just to show how absurdly large $7.2 trillion in money market assets really is.

But here's the real point, the one that actually matters, with rates likely falling soon, that mountain of cash could start chasing better yields elsewhere, which includes high-quality dividend stocks.

Lockheed Martin is a frustrating hold, but its growth rebound, defense tailwinds, and undervaluation make it a prime candidate for a rotation.

Meanwhile, AbbVie continues to deliver with a rock-solid pipeline, smart M&A, and a dividend that's both generous and growing.

Then there's VICI, which is a REIT with a rare moat, a strong position in Las Vegas, and a 5.5% yield that's waiting for lower rates to unlock a lot of value.

The bottom line is that money market funds won't stay this attractive forever.

When the shift happens, these three stocks stand to benefit big-time.

And since this is a significant part of my 'Big Picture', I will share many more ideas in the future, as there are far more ideas than the three presented in this article.

Risks To My Thesis

If the Fed delays rate cuts or trade wars refuel inflation fears, money market yields could stay elevated, slowing the rotation into dividend stocks.

Additionally, weaker-than-expected earnings growth or policy risks (like drug pricing reforms for AbbVie) could pressure investor sentiment for these picks despite their attractive long-term appeal.

data.title: I Held Square Enix Last Fall - Now The Numbers Say It's Time To Exit
data.description:
Thesis

Square Enix Holdings Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SQNXF) (OTCPK:SQNNY) is a Japanese entertainment company best known for turning out legacy franchises like Final Fantasy, Dragon Quest, and Kingdom Hearts. Built on a foundation of role-playing games and media spin-offs, the company has grown into a recognizable fixture across gaming, publishing, merch, and amusement. It’s been around long enough to matter, but that legacy cuts both ways.

On one hand, it's the backbone of the brand. On the other, it's a weight. My analysis argues that recent restructuring efforts may look good in a slide deck: streamlining operations, squeezing more out of old IP, but the core business is still anchored to dated content and a revenue model that’s fallen out of step with where the industry is going. The pivot, while tidy in theory, hasn’t yet shown either the creative shift or financial stability needed to back up the market’s optimistic assumptions.

Square Enix’s Three-Year Reset

What started last fall as my “Hold” call on Square Enix, sustained less by fundamentals than by muscle memory and decades of brand loyalty, has since turned into something closer to a case study in corporate course correction. In October 2024, the picture was far from encouraging. Income was up, sure, but sales were soft, and the gap between perception and performance was widening.

Final Fantasy and Dragon Quest still held their spots on the shelf, but the old aura of untouchability had worn thin. Square Enix wasn’t growing so much as downsizing efficiently, turning a profit not through demand, but by trimming fat, speeding up timelines, and pulling the plug on overstuffed projects. Investors, meanwhile, at that time, seemed hypnotized by the brand, driving up the stock’s price-to-earnings ratio to levels that really didn’t match the underlying reality. It was, in other words, a company coasting on fumes.

Jump ahead, and Square Enix has laid out its response: a three-year plan built around four pillars: clean up the Digital Entertainment division, find new ways to make money, steady the operational base, and try to keep both investors and growth plans fed at the same time.

Start with the numbers, which for once have some real weight behind them. Operating income rose 24.6% year-over-year, from ¥32.56 billion to ¥40.58 billion. Shareholder profit jumped 63.7%, going from ¥14.91 billion to ¥24.41 billion. More to the point, the operating margin edged up from 9.1% to 12.5%, and return on equity moved from 4.7% to 7.5%, not explosive, but enough to suggest the cuts are doing their job.

Take the Digital Entertainment segment, the company’s main act. On paper, it doesn’t add up: net sales sank 16.8%, down to ¥206.53 billion, but operating income in the same category jumped 33%, hitting ¥33.87 billion. The business shrank, and somehow got more profitable.

I'd venture to guess that that kind of split looks like a company that’s finally learning how to squeeze value from its IP without getting buried under its own overhead. Development costs were spread out more carefully, and ad spending was cut by 42.8%. Ad expenses as a share of sales have been dropping steadily from FY2021 to FY2025, a clear sign of a broader effort to tighten the belt.

Breaking the Old Model?

Behind those improved margins is a restructuring tactic that amounts to the company gutting its old setup. Square Enix scrapped its long-standing “Business Unit” system and replaced it with a single “integrated studio” model. Instead of running HD, MMO, and SD games in separate silos, everything now falls under one roof. The goal is smoother workflows and to stop burning time and money on projects that never should’ve gotten past the pitch meeting or that dragged on well past their expiration date.

Tied to the restructuring is a calculated move toward artificial intelligence, and not just for show. It was inevitable that the new AI & Engine Development Division is being plugged directly into the production line, handling everything from quality control to audio and visual workflows. At the same time, the company’s setting up a Human Resources Development Committee and tying bonuses to performance, signaling a shift toward actually managing talent instead of just filling roles.

After going through the business plan, Square Enix, for its part, seems less interested in being just a game studio anymore. The effort to pull in revenue from other angles is starting to take shape. In FY2025, merchandising and licensing picked up, with pop-up stores in Tokyo and L.A. acting as test runs. NieR: Automata, which used to be a niche title, is now showing up as an anime, part of a wider transmedia effort with TBS to stretch its IP beyond games.

Expanding Platform Access

On the platform front, Square Enix is finally stepping away from its old Sony-first playbook. Titles like Final Fantasy XVI are now making their way to Xbox and PC, a long-delayed admission that platform walls don’t mean much anymore. The move into China through code-based sales is riskier, less stable ground, but with upside if it sticks. Meanwhile, PC game revenue in the Digital Entertainment segment has quietly tripled between FY2023 and FY2025. It’s still not the bulk of the business, but it points to a shift away from putting all the weight on console launches.

The Amusement segment doesn’t get much attention, but it’s turned into a steadying presence. Net sales were up 15.7%, reaching ¥71.22 billion, and operating income rose 3.7% to ¥7.85 billion. It’s not game-changing, but it’s consistent.

And maybe the most sensibly handled part of the whole shift is how the company has managed its balance sheet. Cash and cash equivalents swelled by ¥20.43 billion, bringing the total to ¥243.61 billion. The equity ratio nudged its way up from 77.0% to 80.7%, which basically means they’re less reliant on debt and a lot better cushioned if something unexpected comes along. Meanwhile, net assets per share climbed from ¥2,638.33 to ¥2,795.25, a small but clear sign that shareholder value is actually moving in the right direction.

To show they’re thinking of their shareholders, always a good idea, Square Enix has taken a generous turn on dividends. The annual payout per share jumped from ¥38.00 to ¥129.00, which is more than three times what it was the year before, and pushed the payout ratio up to 63.4%.

There’s also a 3-for-1 stock split planned for October 1, 2025, which is the sort of thing companies do when they’d like more people to buy their stock without scaring them off with the price tag. And they’ve set aside ¥80–100 billion over three years for strategic investment.

SQNXF’s Valuation

At $61.22, Square Enix trades at a blended P/E ratio of 42.84x, over three times the sector median of 12.97x and well above its own five-year average. It’s the kind of number that implies either a hidden ace or a collective lapse in judgment, with investors treating a legacy-bound game publisher like it’s reinvented itself as a high-growth tech firm.

Let’s assume, for argument’s sake, that the market is onto something. Maybe it sees the $1.65 billion in cash and zero debt (per Seeking Alpha's data) as the launchpad for a structural pivot, a kind of phoenix moment.

Or maybe it’s extrapolating from the recent 63.61% spike in diluted EPS, a real number, to be sure.

But that EPS surge comes against a five-year compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of -14.94%. Go back a full decade, and the CAGR limps in at just 0.03%.

Still, EPS is just the tip. Revenues dropped by 8.93% year-over-year, falling to ¥324,506 million from ¥356,344 million. In contrast, the broader interactive entertainment sector actually managed a 2.41% gain. Square Enix, instead of riding sector momentum, is swimming against it. And yet, forward projections peg revenue growth at 112.92%. That’s over thirty-four times the sector median. A rational investor might ask; what happened?

The answer, inconveniently, is nothing definitive.

This projected turnaround isn’t backed by a pipeline overhaul, M&A frenzy, or paradigm shift. Instead, it hinges on the hope that the company can recapture the magic of its tentpole IPs, most of which are decades old. The Digital Entertainment segment, which should be the crown jewel, faltered. Badly. A year removed from the launch window of Final Fantasy XVI and Final Fantasy VII Rebirth, the company simply didn’t have the content to drive momentum. The revenue engine sputtered, and with it, the fantasy of durable growth.

Fundamentals Flatten Out

You’d think that with the top line slipping, operating income would follow suit. Instead, it edged up, briefly encouraging, until you look one line down. Ordinary income dropped 1.4%, falling to ¥40,939 million from ¥41,541 million, mostly because last year’s numbers were fluffed up by foreign exchange gains that didn’t show up this time. Strip out the FX sugar high, and what’s left is the plain, unvarnished picture: a business with fundamentals stuck in neutral, if not already drifting in reverse.

The company’s various segments do little to contradict this picture.

The mobile gaming division, once touted as a diversification play, failed to offset core declines. Even the November 2024 launch of Emberstoria couldn’t stave off a dip in revenue and profits. The market is saturated, engagement is down, and the company’s ability to refresh its portfolio appears increasingly strained. Meanwhile, the Publication segment, where Square Enix had seen strength due to anime-adjacent titles like The Apothecary Diaries, posted a 1.1% drop in net sales to ¥30,752 million and an 8.4% drop in operating income to ¥10,977 million.

Now shift to cash flows. Last year, levered free cash flow jumped 100.24%. That would normally be a cause for celebration, except for two things: first, it came alongside a decline in operating cash flow of -18.10%, from ¥52,238 million down to ¥42,781 million; and second, projections for forward cash flow per share show a decline of -8.38%. Add in another projected -6.61% drop in OCF.

Meanwhile, cash out the door for investing rose 14.4% year-over-year, hitting ¥15,114 million. The company’s clearly spending more, but with core cash generation sliding, it looks like a bet to keep from falling further behind.

Final Takeaway

The valuation numbers make the whole thing look absurd. Square Enix trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 15.90x, almost twice the sector average of 8.11x. On an EV/EBIT basis, it's 19.39x compared to 15.55x for the rest of the industry. These kinds of premiums are supposed to reflect explosive growth, fattening margins, or some hidden advantage no one else has. There’s nothing in the company’s track record or short-term plans to back that up.

What we’re left with is a classic case of asset mismatch.

The market’s clinging to the idea that Square Enix has already concluded its medium-term business plan transformation, maybe out of nostalgia, maybe because the algorithms said so. But the numbers show me a company still hooked on old hits, bloated costs, and scattershot performance. The optimism priced into its valuation is borderline delusional. With a projected annual return of -16.85%, this is overvalued.

All things considered, with the stock up over 50% year-to-date, I’d call Square Enix a Sell. Yes, they’ve trimmed the fat and found some margin, but the stock’s price has sprinted way ahead of reality. Revenues are falling, cash flow’s slipping, and the whole operation is still leaning hard on aging franchises.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SK Telecom: A Rare Opportunity For Value Investors
data.description:
We simply attempt to be fearful when others are greedy and to be greedy only when others are fearful. - Warren Buffett

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) learned, in large part thanks to Charlie Munger, that buying great companies at a good price was better than subpar companies at bargain prices. Still, one key problem is that good companies are rarely on sale. Warren Buffett noticed an exception, and that is when these companies get in temporary trouble, or as he likes to put it, "...when they are on the operating table". Some argue one of Buffett's best investments ever was purchasing American Express (AXP) shares during the "salad oil scandal". Stephen R. Foerster revisits the story in detail, and even shares his belief that this was the start of a radical change in Berkshire's and Buffett's investment strategy.

The reason why we bring this up is that SK Telecom (SKM), the leading telecom in South Korea (EWY), experienced a relatively similar situation recently. The company suffered an embarrassing data breach that affected most of the country's population. The company is taking mitigating actions and acknowledging the severity of the incident. We do not think it is as existential as the "salad oil scandal" was to American Express, but it will definitely take a toll over the next few quarters and has further depressed the share price.

“SK Telecom considers this incident the most severe security breach in the company’s history and is putting forth our utmost effort to minimize any damage to our customers,” a spokesperson at SKT told TechCrunch in an emailed statement. “The number of customers affected and the entity responsible for the hacking is under investigation,” the spokesperson added.

When we began coverage of the company, we noted it was trading at a very low valuation despite its leading position in the market, solid balance sheet, attractive dividend yield, and artificial intelligence investments. It appears undervalued even in a country where the overall market trades at a single-digit Price/Earnings multiple. Needless to say, this incident has further lowered the valuation, but there is hope that if the company manages the response in an appropriate fashion it could come out of the situation in good shape. For example, in American Express' case, Buffett recognized the importance of the brand's reputation, and that long-term customer goodwill was more important than trying to minimize short-term impacts.

Buffett felt management was doing the right thing and was concerned that trying to derail any settlement would damage the brand. Buffett sent a letter to Clark, praising management, and imploring them to settle for the sake of reputation. Buffett likened the situation to a typhoon that would soon pass.

In the end, fulfilling its obligations turned out to benefit the American Express brand, with some even later asking the CEO if they should experience a swindle every few years.

Amex executives later realized the swindle was the greatest public-relations event in its history. Press coverage was favorable, and the public saw that Amex stood behind its obligations. In 1964 travelers cheques sales increased by 12 percent. Later Clark was asked if a salad oil swindle every few years might be a clever idea. He replied: “I don’t think I have enough good years of my life to give away for another swindle.”

First Quarter of FY2025 Earnings Results

First quarter results for fiscal year 2025 for SK Telecom were a mixed bag, with positive but low revenue growth, and net income growth also stalling despite a double-digit increase in operating income. Net income attributable to owners of the parent company was up a more respectable 3.2% year-over-year.

To a certain degree, this is unsurprising as South Korea is a country with stagnant population growth, and telecommunications penetration is already very high. This does not mean that there is no hope for SK Telecom to find new growth opportunities. In fact, it has been one of the most aggressive adopters and investors in artificial intelligence. This includes direct investments in AI startups such as Perplexity.ai, as well as services directly offered by the company.

For instance, the AI Data Center [AIDC] segment delivered 11.1% year-over-year revenue growth in the quarter, while AI Experience [AIX] revenue surged by more than 27% in part thanks to strong growth in the AI Cloud business. The AI Infrastructure Data Center segment benefited from the rising demand for GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) subscriptions and is planning the first hyperscale AI data center in Korea by 2027.

The Data Breach

While the cybersecurity incident is no small matter, management deserves credit for quickly entering damage-control mode, isolating affected systems, reinforcing fraud monitoring, promptly suspending new customer sign-ups, and taking protective measures. SK Telecom also decided to temporarily suspend new subscribers until USIM security is fully contained, which might result in the coming quarters still seeing headwinds from the fallout of this issue. Still, we are encouraged by the remarks made during the most recent earnings call from the Head of Marketing Strategy, Jae Woong Yoon, showing understanding that their reputation and customer relationships are critical for the company.

[...] the customer base is the most valuable asset of SK Telecom and a foundation for medium- to long-term profit generation. Therefore, it will be inevitable that we have to bear some expenses to regain the trust of our customers and lock them in.

Currently, we are doing everything we can for customer protection and market stability, and we will continue to closely monitor the market situation and respond accordingly.

The Korean Won

On the positive side, the company operates locally in South Korea, which means that it is less exposed to potential tariff issues, and it is not as sensitive as exporters to a potential appreciation of the South Korean Won. In fact, this could be a tailwind for international investors, as the Korean Won is widely regarded as undervalued at the moment. While it has recently started to appreciate, there is potential for the currency to continue making gains against the U.S. dollar, as Bloomberg recently noted.

Shareholder Return Policy

Dividends are denominated in the Korean Won, which should translate into more dollars if the currency appreciates. Even at prevailing exchange rates the dividend yield is already quite attractive, with shares yielding ~6.85% at current prices. The company has a policy of returning at least 50% of its adjusted net profit for the year on a consolidated basis.

It remains to be seen if the cybersecurity incident will have an impact on the dividend, but when asked by analysts management's reply hints that they will try to avoid impacting dividend distributions with CFO Yang-Seob Kim saying, "Certain levels of financial impact would be inevitable related to the recent incident, but it is rather too early to estimate any potential financial impact with any certainty. So what I can say is that our basic principle of maintaining stable dividends for shareholder returns remains the same."

Future Outlook

The security incident clouds the short to medium-term outlook for the company, but similar to what Buffett described with respect to the Amex "salad oil scandal", it will probably be like a typhoon that will soon pass. We are encouraged that the company appears to be prioritizing maintaining customer goodwill over short-term financial performance. Looking further ahead, we continue to believe that AI growth will become increasingly relevant.

[...] potential impact of this decision on our revenue will very much depend on for how long we will have to suspend new subscriber sign-ups. And so we are doing our best to stabilize the market as quickly as possible. And we will voluntarily disclose to the investors and to the market when we finally decide to resume new sign-ups.

Valuation

It is difficult to believe, but the leading telecom provider in one of the world's most digitally savvy populations is trading at roughly nine times earnings. Of course, the attractive valuation is partly a function of near-term cybersecurity anxiety muddling short-term market sentiment. Still, it is almost impossible to find large companies in the U.S. market with valuation multiples at such extreme lows. An EV/EBITDA multiple of ~3x for an industry leader in a non-cyclical industry is very difficult to find. We are not the only ones to think shares are in deep value territory. For example, Morningstar's (MORN) analyst estimates a fair value of ~$28, even after the cybersecurity incident.

Risks

In the short term, the cybersecurity fallout represents the biggest risk, this issue might affect margins, brand trust, and subscriber counts over the next year, potentially longer if regulators get involved. Longer-term, fierce competition in the South Korean market remains an important risk, with competitors such as KT Corporation and LG Uplus posing a constant challenge across businesses.

There is also some risk that the AI investments might not deliver the expected returns, and the data center build-outs and GPUaaS expansions are not exactly cheap. They can also put pressure on free cash flow, especially should there be delays or cost overruns.

Conclusion

We find some parallels between SK Telecom's cybersecurity incident and American Express' salad oil scandal that allowed Warren Buffett to purchase shares at extremely attractive prices. SK Telecom's cybersecurity incident was definitely unpleasant, but it might have created a potentially very attractive long-term investment opportunity. As Charlie Munger liked to say, "opportunity doesn’t come often, so seize it when it does".

SK Telecom is an industry leader and a good business that appears to have gotten into temporary trouble. We find its response, taking the long-term view and mitigating the impact on customers even if it means lower short-term profits, the best to safeguard the long-term prospects of the company.

data.title: TCW Relative Value Mid Cap Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Environment

Stocks, as represented by the broad-based Russell Midcap, ended the quarter in the negative amid tariff threats combined with heightened concerns of a slowing economy, diminishing consumer confidence, and fears that inflation may reverse course after making much progress toward the Federal Reserve’s (the Fed) 2% goal. Though slower than the prior quarter, the U.S. economy grew by 2.4% in the fourth quarter of 2024. A strong double-digit 4Q:24 earnings season took a backseat to new regime uncertainties. Financials were the best performers followed by Tech+. There was dispersion among the Magnificent 7. Meta† (META) reported beats on both the bottom and top lines while Google’s† (GOOG,GOOGL) stock sold off sharply over concerns that the company’s weaker-than-expected cloud growth and big capex investment. Amazon (AMZN) fell after stating revenue was constrained in its Amazon Web Services division and guidance was less than expected. Nvidia† (NVDA) reported results that topped analysts’ estimates but gave a weaker than expected quarterly forecast for gross margins.

The February jobs report was roughly in line with estimates, and while the unemployment rate ticked up, it remains near historical lows. Average hourly earnings were up 4.0% y/y (as of February) still providing consumers income above inflation. The ISM PMI read for February was a tad over 50 (denoting expansion) for the second consecutive month. However, the new orders and employment components were both back in contraction. While job growth remains solid, and the consumer is in relatively good shape, weak January and February macroeconomic releases stoked concerns the U.S. economy has entered a slow-growth patch which has been exacerbated by recent tariff announcements. Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow estimate model saw a sharp revision to its 1Q:25 GDP forecast from the 3%+ level early in the year to now flat to negative.

As expected, the Fed left the fed funds rate unchanged in March but slowed the pace of its Treasury balance sheet runoff to $5 billion/month from $25 billion to begin April 1 (mortgages are continuing the runoff at the current pace) and kept its forecast for two cuts later this year. It also lowered the U.S. GDP forecast to 1.7% for 2025 and raised inflation to 2.8%. In his comments, Chair Powell said, “We do not need to be in a hurry to adjust our policy stance, and we are well-positioned to wait for greater clarity.” He added that the (U.S.) “economy is strong overall,” “labor market conditions are solid,” and wage growth is outpacing inflation.

Sector Weightings 1

The portfolio sector’s weightings detracted due largely to the overweight in consumer discretionary and underweight in the defensive utilities. On the plus side, the overweights in energy and financials were beneficial. Over the course of the quarter, there was wide dispersion among the eleven major economic sectors. At the top end, the best performers in the Russell Midcap Value were utilities (+8.5%), energy (+6.1%), and consumer staples (+3.3%) while at the bottom, and in the negative, the laggards were information technology (-11.1%), consumer discretionary (-7.4%), and industrials (-6.5%).

Security Selection* 1

The portfolio’s top ten average-weighted names (over the course of the quarter) returned -2.6%, on average, better than the portfolio and benchmark index. The top ten was led by solid gains from Equitable Holdings (EQH; 4.50%**) and Nisource (NI; 3.06%**) and difficult showings from Ares Management (ARES; 2.40%**), Toll Brothers (TOL; 2.74%**), and Flex (FLEX; 3.15%**). The portfolio started underperforming in late February as stocks became highly correlated with tariff fears with few distinctions. The best contribution came from the portfolio’s real estate holdings which gained 6.6% ahead of the group gain of 2.1% led largely by Welltower (WELL; 2.63%**) which rose 22% in the quarter. Welltower leases but does not operate healthcare facilities, therefore it is somewhat immune to the volatile environment. During the quarter, it received several sell-side upgrades and price target increases due to the stability and predictability of its cashflows and dividends. Equitable Holdings’ solid gain stems from a deal with Reinsurance Group of America† that frees up $2 billion in capital that will allow it to focus on strategic investments and stock buybacks in addition to a tender offer for AllianceBernstein†(AB) units. Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM; 2.72%**) outperformed as continued comp momentum was fueled by a differentiated product offering, insulated by an upper income consumer. In addition, a new loyalty program, self-distribution, and continued leverage on its greater scale should continue to drive upward EPS revisions. Utility company NiSource climbed nearly 10% as it raised its earnings forecast due to higher expected electricity demand. Albeit in the negative, the portfolio’s IT names fared better than the group while its energy names topped their peers 8.7% versus 6.1% with Baker Hughes (BKR; 2.38%**) and Range Resources (RRC; 1.39%**) two notable stand outs.

On the downside, the portfolio’s industrials names fell -10.7% behind the group’s -6.5% decline due to Arcosa (ACA; 2.34%**) and United Airlines (UAL; 0.96%**). Arcosa’s stock dropped after reporting both top and bottom lines that missed analysts’ expectations. However, the company had record revenues (+17% y/y) in 2024 and its strategic acquisitions and organic projects completed in the year are expected to bear fruit. United Airlines dropped in concert with the other major airlines as concerns about a global slowdown, recession fears, and ongoing trade retaliation has decreased appetite for travel, both domestic and international. While NiSource was a bright spot, the portfolio’s utilities names as a group underperformed due largely to Sempra (SRE; 1.79%**) whose stock fell as it lowered its 2025 profit forecast amid regulatory challenges (California) and higher cost inflation. The company is now in the process of moving its headquarters to Texas and there has been a slew of insider buying at the lower share price. Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA; 1.18%**) declined after lowering its 2025 guidance due to the IRA’s changes to Medicare Part D as well as a decline in sales from some of its older proprietary drugs. Homebuilder Toll Brothers struggled as it missed on both revenue and earnings as higher borrowing costs and uncertainty are keeping buyers on the sidelines. Ares Management fell in concert with the alternative asset managers (alts) peers as global growth/recessionary concerns slowed deployment and monetization activity. However, during recent meetings, “alts” managements have stated in slowdown scenario, they have ample dry powder to take advantage of forced selling opportunities. IT stocks sold off in the wake of the Deep Seek disruption starting in January and more recently as a result of trade barriers walling off willing buyers and IT contract manufacturer Flex was adversely affected.

Representative Buys (Ticker, Sector)* Advance Auto Parts (AAP; Consumer Discretionary; 0.55%**)

MSCI (MSCI) ESG Score 2: Overall AA

Advance Auto Parts, headquartered in Raleigh, NC., was founded in 1929 as Advance Stores and operated as a retailer of general merchandise. In the 1980s, the company began selling automotive parts and accessories to do it yourself (DIY) customers and then professional installers in 2000. Today, approximately 60% of its sales are generated from professional customers with the rest from DIY. Advance operates approximately 5,000 stores primarily in the U.S. with additional locations in Canada, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands. The company also serves 1,125 independently owned Carquest branded stores. Advance primarily operates three products groups consisting of parts & batteries (63%), accessories & chemicals (22%), and engine maintenance (14%). At initiation, the stock had a market capitalization of $3.0 billion and met four of the five valuation factors (price-to- sales, price-to-cash flow, price-to-book, and above market dividend yield of 2.0%). The catalysts are new management and cost cutting/restructuring initiatives. President/CEO Shane O’Kelly and CFO Ryan Grimsland both joined the company in late 2023.

MSCI ESG Score as of March 31, 2025 and subject to change. Please see additional MSCI ESG disclosures at the end of this document.

Mr. O’Kelly has more than three decades of experience in supply chain and operations with over 15 years at Home Depot† (HD) and as CEO of lubricant distributer PetroChoice. Mr. Grimsland spent nearly two decades at Lowe’s† (LOW). New management’s charge is to oversee a strategic refocus including store closures, supply chain retooling, product line reviews, a reset of the store operating model, and a focus on rebuilding culture within the organization. As a backdrop, the auto parts industry is expected to grow about 3.5% over the next few years with do it for me (DIFM) outpacing DIY due to the growing complexity of parts. The big four aftermarket sellers (AAP, AZO†, NAPA and ORLY†) comprise ~58% of the industry with a shrinking, slower growing pool of independents accounting for the balance. Sector dynamics are also favorable with an expanding, aging, and more complex car park. Transactions are down, but tickets should be supported by rational inflation and a move to more complex, albeit longer lasting, parts. This recent bout of inflation also bolstered the purchase of used cars in the 3-7 age range versus new cars and these cars will be entering the sweet spot for repairs (7-13 years of age) which should provide another tailwind for the sector. Finally, as a percentage of car park electric vehicles will remain relatively low. Given the weight of the batteries in Electric Vehicles (EVs), tires and brakes wear out faster providing a source of replenishment over time benefitting the auto parts sector. In total, we expect these actions to generate top line growth, improve cash flows, margins, and earnings leading to significant stock appreciation over the next two years.

Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR; Consumer Staples; 0.25%**) MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

Dollar Tree, founded in 1986 and headquartered in Chesapeake, VA, is a leading operator of discount variety stores. The company operates 8,881 stores under the Dollar Tree banner which offers merchandise predominantly at the opening price point of $1.25, and Family Dollar which operates 7,622 general merchandise discount stores at multiple price points. On March 26, the company announced the intended sale of the Family Dollar division for $1.0 billion to Brigade Capital Management and Macellum Capital Management, with the deal to close within 90 days. At initiation, the stock had a $16.3 billion market capitalization and meets four of the five valuation factors but does not pay a dividend. Catalysts for the investment include new management, new markets, and restructuring. In December of 2024, Dollar Tree named current Chief Operating Officer and interim CEO, Michael Creedon, Jr. as its permanent CEO, succeeding Rick Dreiling, who stepped down citing health reasons. Before joining Dollar Tree, Mr. Creedon held leadership positions at Advance Auto Parts (EVP and President of Stores), Tyco International, and ADT Security†. In addition, Dollar Tree named Stewart Glendinning as its CFO in March 2025, replacing Jeff Davis. Prior to joining Dollar Tree, Mr. Glendinning served as CEO of Express Inc. and CFO at Tyson Foods (TSN)† and Molson Coors (TAP) Brewing Company†. In terms of expanding new markets, the company introduced $3 and $5 Dollar Tree Plus products in select discretionary categories and later expanded to $3, $4, and $5 frozen and refrigerated products in order to increase customer traffic and store productivity. Management has expanded its multi-price product assortment to 2,600 stores to date. The proposed sale of Family Dollar (FDO) has added a positive restructuring catalyst with a renewed focus on its core Dollar Tree business. Encouragingly, the 2025 guidance calls for an acceleration in comps from 1.8% in 2024 (1.6% increase in traffic and 0.1% price increase) to a forecasted 3-5% rate fueled by the multi-price point upgrade. Risks include Walmart (WMT)† competition and tariff uncertainty. While Walmart continues to execute well and gain market share, the disposition of FDO, means the two retailers are less directly positioned given Dollar Tree’s lower exposure to consumables. As to tariffs, the company initially guided based on the first 10% coming out of China, of which Dollar Tree has mitigated about 90% through a combination of higher price points, vendor negotiations, changing product specifications, eliminating some SKUs, and moving sourcing to other countries. Economic pressures and inflation stemming from increased tariffs could have high end and middle-income consumers trading down into Dollar Tree. Scrubbing the numbers, ~40% of DLTR’s COGS are imported versus 60% sourced domestically resulting in ~32% of total COGS from China. The potential for increased share repurchases is a positive as the company ended 2024 with $1.3 billion and is set to receive $800 million in FDO proceeds, after tax. In total, company catalysts should generate top line growth from a lower base, improve cash flow, margins, and earnings leading to significant stock appreciation over the next two years.

Representative Sells (Ticker Sector)†* AES (AES; Utilities)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall AA

AES, headquartered in Alexandria, VA, is a diversified utility with operations in fourteen countries predominantly in the Americas hemisphere. The company is one of the largest developers of renewables in the U.S. primarily for utility-scale solar + energy storage farms that help corporate customers reduce their carbon footprints. AES also has regulated utility operations in Indiana and Ohio. The catalysts were restructuring and new market opportunities. AES simplified its operations by reducing its country exposure from thirty to fourteen, increased its U.S.-dollar denominated exposure to reduce FX risk, aggressively reduced its coal generation capacity, and developed innovative renewables products for utility-scale energy storage and efficient solar panel installation. We credit AES for its solid project execution as well as for its foresight in securing solar panels ahead of supply chain constraints post pandemic. However, macro pressures started to outweigh excellent execution. In the second half of 2024, the position was reduced as it became more apparent Republicans could win the presidency and Trump’s favoring fossil fuels over renewables. Also, a continued high interest rate environment pressures project costs and it is possible the Inflation Reduction Act is either watered down or repealed under the new administration. These pressures make it unlikely that AES shares will outperform in 2025, and as such the position was eliminated.

Coty (COTY; Consumer Staples) MSCI ESG Score: Overall BB

Coty, headquartered in New York, NY, is one of the world’s largest beauty companies with an iconic portfolio of brands across fragrance, color cosmetics, and skin and body care. COTY is the global leader in fragrance and number three in color cosmetics. Brands include CoverGirl, Rimmel, Gucci, Chloe, and Burberry. COTY’s products are sold in over 150 countries around the world and its brands are committed to a range of social causes as well as seeking to minimize its impact on the environment. Catalysts were new management, new products, and restructuring. COTY struggled to absorb its acquisition of CoverGirl and other brands acquired from Proctor & Gamble† in 2016. After product missteps and several executive changes, Sue Youcef Nabi, ex-CEO of L’Oreal and Lancome and creator of Orveda, was appointed as CEO of COTY in September 2020. Ms. Nabi has a track record of reviving luxury and mass-market brands through product development, brand repositioning, and innovative marketing throughout her 20-year career at L’Oréal. In November 2020, the company sold a 60% majority stake in its Professional and Retail Hair business to Kolberg Kravis Roberts & Co.† (KKR (KKR)) for net proceeds of $2.4 billion. The sale of a majority interest in this business simplified COTY’s portfolio and allows it to focus on its core Prestige and Mass Beauty businesses. KKR also made a $1 billion investment in COTY via convertible preferred shares and named two directors to COTY’s board. Under this new leadership team, COTY is now focused on executing on a $800 million fixed cost reduction program between 2021 and 2025 and outlined initiatives to accelerate revenue growth via the premiumization of its portfolio. Initiatives under the plan include the stabilization of Mass Beauty fragrances and make-up, acceleration of Luxury fragrances and Prestige make-up, building a skincare portfolio in Prestige and Mass, enhancing e-commerce capabilities, and expanding in China through its Prestige and Consumer Beauty brands. Despite strong execution and improved financial metrics, macro impacts of a pressured consumer have increased. The deteriorating market sentiment on the beauty industry is a new overhang on the COTY stock and company leverage of 3.9x (down from 6.4x under Ms. Nabi) remains above its peers’. Therefore, we decided to exit the position.

Guess (GES; Consumer Discretionary) MSCI ESG Score: Overall BBB

Guess Inc., headquartered in Los Angeles, CA, designs, markets, distributes, and licenses a collection of apparel, denim, handbags, watches, footwear, and other related consumer products. Its products are distributed through branded Guess stores and department and specialty stores in approximately 100 countries worldwide. On April 2, 2024, Guess acquired all the operating assets and a 50% interest in the intellectual property assets of New York-based fashion brand rag & bone, a leader in the American fashion scene, directly operating stores in the U.S. and in the U.K., and available in high-end boutiques, department stores, and through e-commerce globally. As of November 2, 2024, the company directly operated 1,057 retail stores in Europe, the Americas, and Asia. In addition, Guess’s partners and distributors operated 541 additional retail stores worldwide. At exit, Guess had a market capitalization of $507 million and met all valuation factors with a dividend yield of 12.2%. Guess presented a five-year strategic plan in 2019 which included a focus on organization and culture, brand relevancy, product excellence, customer centricity, its global footprint, and functional capabilities. The quantitative part of this plan includes the goal of achieving an operating margin of 10% by fiscal 2025, with net revenues of $2.9 billion, and a 2% CAGR from fiscal 2020. Although the company is on track to achieve the $2.9 billion in revenues, this includes the recent acquisition of rag & bone, without which the top line goal likely would not have been met. In addition, Guess is not on track to achieve its operating margin goal of 10% and is coming in closer to 6.2-6.5%. While Guess has made impressive progress in realigning its North American footprint and brand and expanding revenues in Europe and Asia, the brand health in North America remains in question in terms of its long-term viability. This, combined with not being to meet its long-term operating margin goal, resulted in our decision to eliminate the position.

Market Outlook

We came into the year with expectations that the U.S. economy would continue to grow at around a 2% growth rate and that corporate profits (earnings per share) would advance this year at a rate in excess of 10%. EPS forecasts have been reduced to the high single digits and, in some cases, to the low single digits (3-4% per Goldman Sachs (GS), 3/31/25) due to the tariff economic drags. Surveys of CEOs and CFOs show that confidence has declined.

“Liberation Day” arrived after the close Wednesday, April 2nd and stocks globally sunk on back-and-forth retaliations with the biggest adversary and our largest importer, China. The onset of the trade war overshadowed the pretty good March jobs data. Even though the unemployment rate ticked up a notch to 4.2%, it is still near historical lows. Average hourly earnings y/y are up 3.8%, down from the prior month read of 4.0% but still outpacing inflation. While backward looking macroeconomic data suggests a still resilient U.S. economy, most indicators point to a significant slowdown. U.S. stock markets are one of the most important leading economic indicators. It is typical for the broad market to be down 3-5% four or five times a year, but uncertainty is increasing with severe potential economic implications.

Global manufacturing remains sluggish. While China’s Manufacturing PMI improved 0.4 ppts to 51.2, the highest since November, most other countries are in contraction territory. Indeed, global manufacturing PMIs show that one just one-third of the major PMIs are in expansion territory. This is the lowest level since September 2024. The weak level is notable. The last time we saw a trade war, manufacturing started from a much stronger base with much stronger momentum than it is now. There is less of an economic buffer this time around.

We are maintaining our “steady hand” with overall risk controls such as position sizes and sector guidelines and keeping eyes on the company specific catalysts that we believe will work in most macro environments. Our view is that it would be just like President Trump to start with big announcements but leave the door open for “negotiations.” April could mark the lows, and seasonally, second quarters are the next best performance months other than Q4. Europe has the potential to keep outperforming the U.S., but there are a lot of geopolitical risks to be considered there as well. Value should continue to outperform the broad

U.S. market for the rest of the year, but it is never a straight line.

If current events lead to a liquidity crisis, there is no doubt the U.S. Federal Reserve board will step in although it would be better for everyone if it doesn’t come to that. A Reuters/Ipsos poll published on April 8 found that a majority (57%) of American adults oppose President Trump’s new tariffs so perhaps cooler heads will prevail. The Relative Value team reviewed tariff implications starting last February when it became clear President Trump was to be the Republican presidential nominee. While the breadth and the magnitude of the current tariffs are greater than the macro scenario testing, the portfolio was scrubbed last year, and the direction taken was correct. In addition, all holdings are continuously tested to meet minimum balance sheet and liquidity requirements in the event of a recession which would be the outcome of tariff demand destruction. It is mitigated by most companies shifting to local production for local demand following Trump 1.0 and revamping supply chains post pandemic. Technology and consumer discretionary goods are likely to be the most negatively affected but offer the most upside if negotiations and long-term policies are put in place. More domestic providers of healthcare, utilities, and food products should be the least impacted. The Relative Value diversification strategy of representing all 11 major economic sectors should prove beneficial over time.

We are busy and actively working to make the best portfolio decisions possible with the information available and will adjust should the “facts” change. We are constructive about the future but maintain strong risk controls for turbulent events such as these.

Diane E. Jaffee, CFA, Group Managing Director

Iman H. Brivanlou, PhD, Managing Director
